article.title: NASA's Artemis II Lifts Off for the Moon, Sending Humans Beyond Earth Orbit for the First Time in 53 Years
article.summary: Four astronauts launched aboard the Space Launch System from Kennedy Space Center on April 1, beginning a ten-day lunar flyby that will test the Orion spacecraft before future landing missions.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA's Space Launch System rocket lifted off from Launch Complex 39B at Kennedy Space Center on April 1, carrying four astronauts toward the Moon aboard the Orion spacecraft. The Artemis II mission marks the first time humans have traveled beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in December 1972, opening a new chapter in lunar exploration after years of delays, technical setbacks, and a [restructured Artemis program](/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis-program-adds-new-mission-and-pushes-moon-landing-to-2028).

The launch window opened at 6:24 p.m. EDT with an [80 percent chance of favorable weather](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/), and ground teams spent the day executing thousands of commands through the ground launch sequencer as [tanking operations began at 7:45 a.m. EDT](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/). The 322-foot rocket, [the most powerful operational booster in the world](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/), generated nearly 9 million pounds of thrust during an eight-minute climb to orbit.

This follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/29-artemis-ii-crew-arrives-at-kennedy-space-center-as-countdown-begins-for-first-crewed-lunar-voyage-in-53-years) on the crew's arrival at Kennedy Space Center and the start of the launch countdown.

## The Crew

The four-person crew represents several historic firsts. Commander Reid Wiseman, a former Navy fighter pilot with [165 days of International Space Station experience](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1), leads the mission. Pilot Victor Glover, who flew on SpaceX Crew-1, becomes [the first Black astronaut assigned to a lunar mission](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch). Mission Specialist Christina Koch holds [the record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman at 328 days](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch) and will be the first woman to travel to lunar distance.

Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen rounds out the crew as [the first Canadian to leave Earth orbit](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/) and the first non-American on a lunar mission. The flight also marks Hansen's maiden spaceflight. The crew named their Orion capsule ["Integrity"](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## The Mission

Artemis II follows a free-return trajectory, swinging around the far side of the Moon without entering lunar orbit. After launch, the crew will spend approximately [24 hours in Earth orbit](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/) testing life support systems, waste management, communications, navigation, and propulsion before the upper stage fires to send them toward the Moon.

The spacecraft will approach within [roughly 4,100 miles of the lunar surface](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/), and depending on the exact launch time, the crew could [travel as far as 252,000 miles from Earth](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/), surpassing Apollo 13's distance record of 248,655 miles. The ten-day journey will cover [approximately 685,000 miles](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1), with splashdown in the Pacific Ocean targeted for April 10.

The crew will also become the [first humans to directly observe large portions of the Moon's far side illuminated by sunlight](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## The Heat Shield Question

The mission's highest-profile technical concern centers on the Orion capsule's AVCOAT heat shield. During the uncrewed Artemis I mission in late 2022, the shield experienced unexpected [uneven erosion during reentry](https://theconversation.com/heat-shield-safety-concerns-raise-stakes-for-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-275853), with chunks of ablative material shedding in patterns not predicted by pre-flight models.

NASA's root cause investigation determined that [irregular gas releases trapped within the heat shield material, compounded by the skip reentry profile](https://theconversation.com/heat-shield-safety-concerns-raise-stakes-for-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-275853), caused the cracking. Between atmospheric dips, thermal energy accumulated inside the AVCOAT, building pressure that created uneven material loss.

Rather than replacing the heat shield, NASA [updated the block design to allow gases to escape more effectively and abandoned the skip reentry in favor of a direct reentry mode](https://theconversation.com/heat-shield-safety-concerns-raise-stakes-for-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-275853). The direct reentry reduces heating uncertainty but increases deceleration forces on the crew. The capsule's 16.5-foot-wide heat shield must withstand [temperatures approaching 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/) during reentry at speeds [slightly exceeding 25,000 mph](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1).

## What We Don't Know

This is the first crewed flight of the Orion capsule, meaning its life support, carbon dioxide scrubbing, and waste management systems have never been tested with humans aboard. The mission is fundamentally [a systems validation flight](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch), designed to identify problems under real deep-space conditions that cannot be replicated on Earth.

Canadian astronaut Hansen acknowledged the inherent risks during pre-launch preparations, stating that on a test flight of this nature, ["you can lose a crew. And if we do, that shouldn't shock us"](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

The mission also faced [two significant delays](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch) earlier this year: a liquid hydrogen leak in February and a helium flow issue in March that pushed the launch from its original schedule.

## What Comes Next

Artemis II is the second step in NASA's restructured lunar campaign. Under the [Ignition initiative announced in late March](/article/2026-03/25-nasa-pauses-lunar-gateway-and-unveils-20-billion-moon-base-plan-as-ignition-initiative-redirects-agency-toward-surface-operations-and-nuclear-powered-mars-probe), Artemis III has been transformed into an [orbital test flight scheduled for 2027](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch), with the first crewed lunar surface landing now targeted for Artemis IV in early 2028. A second landing mission, Artemis V, is planned for [late 2028](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch).

The broader program unfolds within geopolitical competition, as [the United States seeks to maintain leadership in space exploration amid growing rivalry with China](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch), which has announced plans for its own crewed lunar landing by 2030.
